trajectory_summary: The call moves efficiently through verify and reaches a tentative appointment, but the outcome is structurally fragile. The bill-collection thread is never resolved — the appointment is explicitly gated on Raymond retrieving a bill he doesn't manage, from a wife who wasn't included in the call. The rep's bill-swap reframe partially recovered from a self-seeded skepticism moment, but no emotional flip was deployed and the spouse was not included in the appointment. The call ends with energy that looks like a booking but carries the structural vulnerabilities of a conditional one.
outcome_dispute: V1 tag is unknown; recommend classifying as conditional_booking based on observed evidence. The rep penciled in a 5PM slot but explicitly conditioned confirmation on bill delivery ('then I can get you guys on the on the schedule here' at 06:33). The wife is on the title and was not included. These two factors together make this appointment structurally fragile — it is not a firm sit.

primary_coaching_focus.quote: That's not my department. This is my wife.
primary_coaching_focus.why: The wife is confirmed on the title at 02:10 and identified as the bill manager at 03:32 — two separate signals that she is a co-decision-maker. The rep acknowledged neither. Per protocols.spouse_decision, the correct move is to include her in the appointment, not defer to 'get with your wife' at close. This single gap makes the appointment structurally fragile: a closer who walks into a proposal with only one title-holder present is working against the deal from the start. In the 1-on-1, replay 02:10 and 03:32 together and ask the rep: 'At what point did you know the wife was on the title? What would you say at 03:32 to make sure she's on the call?' The goal is for the rep to internalize the trigger — not to recite a rule, but to recognize the moment.

key_moments.stewart_read: The rep calls the customer 'Raymond' in the opening question, then immediately introduces himself as 'Randy' — his own name. Per the schema, legitimacy in the first 30 seconds is a primary success signal. A name confusion at the intro, even minor, introduces a small but real friction: the customer has to parse whether the rep knows who he's calling. Raymond's 'Yeah. Who's speaking?' at 00:10 suggests he was already slightly uncertain. The rep recovered quickly, but the opener was not clean.

key_moments.quote: There's actually no upfront or out of pocket cost whatsoever.
key_moments.stewart_read: The rep volunteers 'no upfront or out of pocket cost whatsoever' unprompted, before Raymond has raised any cost concern. Per the schema's curiosity_over_features anti-pattern, proactively selling features — especially cost claims — creates objections rather than curiosity. Raymond's response at 03:47 ('some people get them for free. I don't believe that') is a direct downstream consequence: the rep's 'free' framing seeded the skepticism it then failed to resolve. The rep did not deploy an escape hatch or a flip — instead offered a vague 'it really just depends' before pivoting to the bill-swap reframe.

key_moments.quote: Oh, I think we just had somebody come out, and they said we got another ten ten years left. So I'm gonna assume twenty years.
key_moments.stewart_read: Raymond's answer signals a roof that is approximately 10–15 years old already (inspector says 10 years left; Raymond estimates 20 years total age). The schema hard-DQ threshold is roof_age_over_15_no_replacement_planned. The rep accepted the estimate without probing — no follow-up on whether Raymond has a roof replacement planned or budgeted. This is simultaneously a qualification risk and a missed cross-sell signal: if the roof is approaching end-of-life, Ion's in-house roofing team is the correct route, and a roof-plus-solar conversation may be more valuable than a solar-only appointment. The rep's silence here is a reactive miss.

key_moments.quote: That's not my department. This is my wife.
key_moments.stewart_read: Raymond explicitly names his wife as the person who manages the bills at 03:32 — and she is already confirmed on the title at 02:10. This is a double signal: the wife is both a co-decision-maker and the gatekeeper for the bill the rep needs. The rep's response is a social laugh-off ('Oh, yeah. Yeah.') with no move to include the wife in the appointment. Per protocols.spouse_decision, the correct response is to include the spouse in the appointment — not to overcome the mention as an objection, but to route the appointment to include her. The rep instead defers to 'get with your wife' at 06:33, which leaves both the bill and the decision-maker outside the appointment structure.
